title: Chapter Five: The Tape Measure
content: The doorman knows my name.

"Ms. Voss," he says, holding the door with a gloved hand, and I wonder how — whether Rowan Ashworth told him to expect me, or whether there's a list, or whether the doormen of Upper East Side limestone townhouses simply know things the way butlers in novels always know things: intimately, completely, without being told.

The lobby smells of beeswax polish and old money. Dark wainscoting. A brass elevator with a gate instead of doors. The operator takes me up in silence, which is a relief, because I'm rehearsing a single sentence on loop: *This is a job. Just a job.*
Suki's voice in my ear this morning: *Don't let him get in your head.* Easy for her to say. She doesn't carry centuries in her bones like sediment.

The fitting room is on the fourth floor. It's bigger than my entire studio — bigger than my studio and Suki's studio combined, probably, with room left over for a small orchard. Full-length mirror in a gilded frame. A velvet divan in deepest burgundy, soft as a bruise. Skylight pouring afternoon light across everything like honey. I set my kit on the mahogany table and spread my tools with ritual precision: tape measure, yellow chalk, silk pins, shears. Each one a small, familiar weight. Each one a promise of control.

I am good at this. I am *very* good at this. The body is a series of measurements, a geometry of planes and angles, and I have been reading bodies for longer than anyone alive. I can take a man's measure without touching his skin. I can map a silhouette from across a room.
*This is a job. Just a job.*
He enters without knocking, and the room changes.

That's the only way I can describe it. The air pressure shifts. The quality of light rearranges itself around him, as though the skylight were saving its best angles for the moment Rowan Ashworth stepped through the door.

Grey crew neck, sleeves pushed to the elbows. Dark trousers. The emerald ring on his right hand, catching light. In the photograph from the magazine, he'd looked composed — handsome, certainly, but containable within a frame. In person he is *larger*. Not taller than the photograph promised, not broader exactly, but *more*. He fills the room simply by being in it, the way a single note can fill a cathedral.

"Ms. Voss." He doesn't offer his hand. He looks at my tools on the table and something flickers across his face — curiosity, maybe, or recognition of their intimacy. A stranger's hands on your body is always intimate, even when it's clinical. Especially when it's clinical.
"Mr. Ashworth." I gesture to the fitting block. "If you'd stand here."
He sits instead. Sits on the low wooden block and looks up at me, and his eyes — I hadn't noticed in the photograph — are the grey-green of river stones, wet and ancient.
"Whenever you're ready," he says.

I begin with his shoulders. The tape measure is a familiar friend, a yellow ribbon of certainty, and my hands are steady as I drape it from shoulder point to shoulder point. Eighteen and three-eighths inches. I write the number on my pad. His chest: forty-two and change, the fabric of his crew neck warm from his body, the faint solidity of muscle beneath. I keep my movements efficient, impersonal, the way I've done a thousand times. Waist. Hips. Inseam — he shifts his weight, I adjust, professional as surgery. Sleeve length. Back width. Nape to waist.
My hands do not shake. My hands *never* shake.

I move behind him to position the collar point. This is standard — I need the base of his neck, the seventh cervical vertebra, the place where a collar will sit and breathe and move with him. My fingers find the knob of his spine, and I press gently to mark the spot, and—
—I am in Venice.
The room is small and candlelit and the air is thick with the smell of rosewater and sickness and the canal is close, I can hear water lapping against stone, and my arms—my *arms*—
My arms are holding a man.

He is in my lap and he is burning with fever and I am Orsino, I am Orsino Valieri, I have a man's body, a man's hands, a man's chest that aches with the weight of holding him, and his name is *Marco*.
Marco.

His cheekbones catch the candlelight. They are high and precise, carved like the angels on the Basilica, and his skin is the color of wet paper and his breath rattles in his chest like a music box failing, and I am holding him and I am *shaking* because the body I am in is strong and it cannot save him and that is the cruelest joke — a strong body that cannot stop a weak one from leaving.
"Don't leave me," Marco says.
His voice is sand and salt and the green ring on his finger catches the candlelight and I can *feel* it, I can feel the emerald pressing into my palm where I grip his hand, and I—
"I won't," I say. "I won't, I won't, I'm here—"

And I am lying. I am lying because the priest came this morning and the blood is on the handkerchief and his fingers are cold inside my grip and I am a man who loves a man in a city that would drown us both for it if they knew, and none of that matters, *none of it matters*, because he is dying and I said I wouldn't leave and I am *lying*—
His eyes. His eyes are grey-green. River stones.
The candle gutters.
The last breath is a sound like a page turning.

And the body remembers. The body I wore then, the arms that held him, the chest that cracked open — the body *remembers*, and it doesn't care that it's three hundred years later, it doesn't care that the shape is different now, it doesn't care that I have buried this so deep that even the grave has forgotten. The body remembers the way water remembers the shape of the riverbed even when the river runs dry.
The tape measure clatters to the floor.

My hand is on his neck. My fingers are still pressed to the base of Rowan Ashworth's skull and I am *gasping* — a raw, ugly sound, like I've surfaced from deep water, from a canal, from a candlelit room in a city that sunk beneath the sea.
Rowan catches my wrist.

Instinct. Not calculation. His fingers close around my wrist and hold, firm, and I can feel the emerald ring pressing cold against my pulse point and the hum — the *hum* — it's deafening, it's the sound of a basilica bell, it's water against stone, it's three centuries collapsing into a single point of contact.
"Are you alright?"

His voice is close. His face is inches from mine. His pupils are dilated — blown wide, black swallowing grey-green, and I can see my own reflection in them, fractured and strange, and he can feel my pulse hammering under his fingers like a trapped bird because his thumb is right *there*, right on the vein, and he is looking at me the way—
—the way Marco looked at me when he said *don't leave me*—

I pull back. My whole arm is trembling. The hum doesn't stop, it rings through my skeleton like a struck bell, and I take one step, two steps away, and my hip hits the fitting table and the silk pins scatter like tiny silver fish.
"I'm fine." My voice is a stranger's. "Low blood sugar. I forgot to eat this morning."

The lie is so transparent it's almost funny. I can taste adrenaline on my tongue, metallic and sharp, and underneath it something older — grief, actual grief, the taste of rosewater and candle smoke and a canal at night. I can smell the beeswax polish of this room and the ghost of another room, simultaneously, layered like tissue paper.
Rowan doesn't call it out.

He lets go of my wrist slowly. His fingers uncurl one by one, and there's a moment — a fraction of a fraction — where his hand lingers at the edge of release, where his palm grazes the inside of my wrist, and something *passes* between us. Something that doesn't have a name in any language I've spoken across any lifetime.
He looks at his own hand like it's betrayed him. Then he folds it into a fist and sets it on his knee.
"Take your time," he says. Quiet. Steady.
I pick up the tape measure from the floor. It takes me two tries.
The rest of the fitting happens in silence.
Not hostile silence. Not the silence of people avoiding each other. The silence of people who have touched something electric and are being very, very careful not to touch it again.

I use the tape measure as a barrier. When I need to mark a point, I press the tape between my fingers and his body — yellow vinyl, not skin. When I pin the muslin, I pin through fabric and let the fabric touch him, my hands hovering above the cloth like a surgeon's above an open cavity. Every touch is a negotiation. Every inch of proximity is a risk I am managing.

He doesn't speak. He holds still, a patience in his stillness that feels ancient — like he's been measured before, not for clothing, but for something else. A coffin, maybe. A frame. He holds still the way a man holds still when he knows the smallest movement could change everything and he hasn't decided yet whether he wants it to change.

I measure his wrist. Seven and a quarter. I mark the cuff point on the muslin. I chalk the center back. I write numbers on my pad and the numbers are correct — my hands are shaking but the numbers are *correct*, because this is the one thing I can do, the one thing that has never failed me across every body I've worn and every city I've lived in and every person I've lost: I can read a body. I can translate it into geometry. I can make it plans and patterns and pieces that will fit.
I cannot make it stay.
"You're very good at that," he says, finally. His voice is rough, like he's forgotten how to use it.
"It's my job." I snap the tape measure into its case. "I'll have the first muslin ready in ten days. Second fitting here, same time?"
"Same time."

I pack my tools. Chalk, pins, shears. Each one returned to its place in the leather case. The ritual of it steadies me — the click of the clasp, the zip of the pocket, the weight of the kit in my hand. Familiar. Contained. *Just a job.*
He walks me to the door.

I don't ask him to. He simply rises and follows, and when we reach the threshold of the fitting room — the space where candles become skylight, where canals become elevators, where Venice becomes the Upper East Side — he stops.
I stop too, because I can feel him not-speaking, the way you can feel a storm holding its breath.
"You looked at me," he says, "like you'd lost me before."
The words hit me in the sternum. The body remembers. The body *always* remembers. And now the mind can't pretend it didn't.

I don't turn around. I can feel his eyes on my back, on the nape of my neck, on the place where his fingers pressed the hum into my blood less than an hour ago and it hasn't stopped, it hasn't stopped, it won't stop—
"Maybe I have."

I walk out. The elevator operator takes me down. The doorman holds the door. The June air hits my face and it's warm and it's 2026 and I am Orlando Voss in a woman's body in a woman's clothes and my hands are steady on the leather kit and my body is *shaking*.
I get four blocks before I have to stop.

I lean against the window of a boutique I can't see and I press my palm flat to the glass and I breathe. In and out. In and out. The hum settles from a shriek to a drone to a low, persistent vibration in my marrow. Manageable. Survivable.
The body remembers.
And now, for the first time in three hundred years — so does he.
